question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted transaction body that causes an unclassified error (not NotFoundError, InvalidArgumentError, DbError, or HttpError) to fall through to defaultStatusCode (INTERNAL_ERROR 500), causing the server to return a generic error that masks the true tampered transaction state and prevents the client from detecting the tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction that triggers an HttpError with an attacker-controlled err.statusCode and err.msg, causing handleError() to construct a new StatusCode(err.statusCode, err.msg) with arbitrary values, allowing the attacker to manipulate the HTTP response code returned to other clients observing the transaction result?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction whose error object contains a crafted message with a 'detail' property, causing errorMessageFormat() to expose both m.message and m.detail in the _status.messages response, leaking internal transaction processing details that reveal how the tampering was detected?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction error that includes a 'data' property on the error message object, causing errorMessageFormat() to include response['data'] = m.data in the JSON response, leaking arbitrary internal data fields attached to the transaction error back to the attacker?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: shouldReturnMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that triggers a 4xx client error (statusCode.isClientError() returns true), causing shouldReturnMessage() to return true and expose the full err.message to the attacker, revealing internal validation logic that can be used to refine the tampering attack?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: shouldReturnMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction that triggers a BAD_GATEWAY (502) response, causing shouldReturnMessage() to return true (statusCode === httpStatusCodes.BAD_GATEWAY), thereby leaking the full upstream error message that reveals backend transaction routing details?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: shouldPrintStacktrace()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction whose error message contains the string 'statement timeout', causing shouldPrintStacktrace() to return false via the simpleErrors regex, suppressing the stacktrace log and allowing the attacker to silently tamper with transactions while reducing forensic evidence in server logs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user inject newline characters or CRLF sequences into a tampered transaction's URL or parameters, causing req.originalUrl to contain log-injection payloads that are written to the logger via the template literal `${req.ip} ${req.method} ${req.originalUrl}`, corrupting audit logs and hiding evidence of transaction tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an InvalidArgumentError, and by observing the exact err.message returned in the 400 response (since shouldReturnMessage returns true for 4xx), enumerate which specific transaction fields are validated, enabling iterative refinement of the tampered transaction to bypass validation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that triggers a RangeError (caught by err instanceof RangeError in handleError), causing a 400 BAD_REQUEST response with the full RangeError message exposed, leaking numeric boundary information about transaction field limits that aids in crafting a precisely tampered transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleUncaughtException()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that triggers a non-RestError uncaught exception, causing handleUncaughtException() to re-throw the error (throw err), crashing the Node.js process and causing a denial-of-service that prevents legitimate transactions from being processed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleUncaughtException()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction that triggers a RestError subclass (DbError, InvalidArgumentError, NotFoundError, etc.) as an uncaught exception, causing handleUncaughtException() to silently log it without crashing, allowing the attacker to repeatedly tamper with transactions while the server continues operating without alerting operators?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleRejection()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an unhandled promise rejection, causing handleRejection() to log `Unhandled rejection at:${promise} reason: ${reason}` with attacker-controlled content in the reason field, injecting arbitrary strings into server logs to obscure transaction tampering evidence?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes a DbError, receiving a 503 SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE response, and use the timing of this response (via elapsed = Date.now() - startTime) to perform a timing side-channel attack that infers whether the tampered transaction reached the database layer?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction where the error object's constructor.name is a very long string, causing the logger.warn template literal `${err.constructor.name}` to write an excessively large log entry, leading to log storage exhaustion and denial-of-service that hides transaction tampering activity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that generates an error with a non-array, non-string message object (e.g., a plain object without a 'detail' property), causing errorMessageFormat() to wrap it in {message: m} where m is an object, resulting in a malformed JSON response that confuses downstream clients parsing transaction error responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger an error path where err.message is an array (as produced by InvalidArgumentError.forParams()), causing errorMessageFormat() to iterate over each element and expose multiple validation failure messages simultaneously, revealing the complete set of transaction field constraints to an attacker refining a tampered transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an HttpError with a statusCode in the 4xx range, causing handleError() to create StatusCode(err.statusCode, err.msg) and shouldReturnMessage() to return true (isClientError()), thereby exposing the full HttpError message including any internal routing or proxy details?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes res.locals[requestStartTime] to be undefined (e.g., by bypassing the requestLogger middleware), causing elapsed to be 0 in handleError(), which may indicate to the attacker that the request bypassed normal timing instrumentation and reveal a middleware bypass path?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that triggers a NotFoundError, and by observing the 404 response with the full err.message (since shouldReturnMessage returns true for 4xx), enumerate which transaction IDs or resources exist in the system, enabling targeted transaction tampering against known resources?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: shouldPrintStacktrace()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction whose error message is crafted to match the simpleErrors regex (/statement timeout/), causing shouldPrintStacktrace() to return false and preventing the full stack trace from being logged, reducing the forensic trail of the tampering attempt in server logs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message is a very large string (e.g., megabytes), causing handleError() to include it in the JSON response via errorMessageFormat() and logger.warn(), leading to memory exhaustion or response size amplification that degrades service for legitimate transaction submissions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error with a circular reference in err.message, causing res.status(statusCode.code).json(errorMessageFormat(errorMessage)) to throw a JSON serialization error, resulting in an unhandled exception that crashes the response pipeline and leaves the transaction in an indeterminate state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a tampered transaction error where m is an object with both 'detail' and 'data' properties, causing errorMessageFormat() to include all three fields (message, detail, data) in the response, leaking the maximum amount of internal error context about why the tampered transaction was rejected?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a tampered transaction that causes an error where err.message is null or undefined, causing handleError() to log `${errorMessage}` as 'undefined' or 'null' and return it in the JSON response, potentially causing downstream clients to misinterpret the error as a successful transaction?
